evidence_quotes.quote: Some modern titles are designed in a way that the publisher can revoke our access to our game whenever they please as stated in their EULAs or other agreements.

In digital storefronts the big green buttons always say "buy" as if we are getting a product that we own - it is never transparently stated to the consumer that it is only a license for access, that can be revoked at any time.
There are no refunds for taking away the product that you bought;

paragraphs.text: Please also consider the extremely anti-consumer practice of turning off and therefore "destroying" video games already sold to consumers. Some modern titles are designed in a way that the publisher can revoke our access to our game whenever they please as stated in their EULAs or other agreements. When purchasing the video game it is never stated how long it is going to last - months later it may be taken away, without any refund to the buyer. In digital storefronts the big green buttons always say "buy" as if we are getting a product that we own - it is never transparently stated to the consumer that it is only a license for access, that can be revoked at any time. It is an extremely predatory practice that also hits the matter of game preservation - these live service games are often built to be turned off as soon as the profit is not worth it. More often than not, these games could be played offline in single-player mode, but are designed in such a way that is solely dependent on the publisher. There are no refunds for taking away the product that you bought; there is not transparency in whether we are buying games or licenses; there is no end-of-life plans for many video games. They are being designed as quick cash-grabs that hit the consumers and the matter of entertainment history preservation. Thank you for your time.
